us-politics.headline: US-Iran War Escalates as Airman Rescued, Hormuz Threatened
us-politics.body: Trump threatened to bomb Iranian power plants and bridges on Easter Sunday if Iran does not reopen the Strait of Hormuz, using unusually blunt language in his public warning. The US military simultaneously rescued a service member whose jet was shot down over Iran, a rare piece of good news amid an intensifying conflict. A retired Army general predicted the war could conclude in under 60 days, though analysts remain divided on that timeline. The economic fallout is already spreading. Jet fuel costs have spiked sharply, forcing airlines to cancel flights and raise fares. A former World Bank chief warned Iran's economy is collapsing toward a barter system under the pressure of sustained conflict and sanctions.

sources.title: Trump drops Easter Sunday f-bomb in new threat to Iran
Trump vows US will strike Iran’s power plants, bridges if Strait of Hormuz is not reopened
US rescues missing service member from downed jet in Iran: Trump
Retired Army general predicts Iran war wrapped up in ‘less than 60 days’
Jet fuel costs skyrocket amid Iran war, exacerbating crisis for airlines, travelers
Trump says U.S. military has rescued airman shot down over Iran
Former World Bank chief says Iran economy collapsing into ‘barter system’ amid conflict

us-politics.headline: Trump Fires Bondi, Courts Face Off Over Education Data
us-politics.body: Trump fired Attorney General Pam Bondi, a move NPR framed alongside reports that Trump is walking back several campaign promises. The White House has not offered a detailed public explanation for the dismissal. Separately, a federal judge blocked the administration's push to require colleges to submit race-based admissions data, ruling against a policy critics said was designed to expose and penalise affirmative action practices. Supporters of the administration argued the data collection was a legitimate transparency measure. The ruling adds to a growing list of judicial checks on executive action, which critics across the political spectrum have flagged as an overuse of emergency and executive powers.

sources.title: Politics chat: Trump fires Attorney General Bondi, seeks to break campaign promises
Federal judge blocks Trump push to collect race-based admissions data
Judge blocks Trump bid to require colleges to prove race not considered in admissions
Trump’s abuse of emergency powers is the real national emergency

us-politics.headline: Alliance Fractures, Hungary Vote, and the Pope's Peace Call
us-politics.body: Spain's decision to ban US military aircraft from its airspace has prompted debate about whether NATO's era of universal collective action is ending. Analysts describe a drift toward transactional, bilateral arrangements replacing the bloc's traditional unity. In Hungary, Trump and Putin are both backing Viktor Orban ahead of a closely watched election, a rare alignment that has drawn scrutiny from European governments. Pope Leo used his Easter address to deliver a pointed call for peace, with the Washington Post noting the message carried implicit weight given active US military operations. The speech drew wide attention but no formal response from the White House.

sources.title: The ‘post-global’ NATO: Spain’s airspace ban signals the death of universal alliances
In Hungarian election, Trump and Putin are backing Viktor Orban
On Easter, Pope Leo delivers commanding message of peace to a world at war

us-politics.headline: ICE Mask Fight, Election Ads, and Harris Eyes 2028
us-politics.body: Black Democratic lawmakers are pushing back against ICE agents wearing face masks during enforcement operations, drawing on historical arguments about accountability and anonymous state power. Republicans have defended the practice as a safety measure for agents. A new ad campaign backed by Republicans is targeting Trump's push to federalise election administration, arguing it threatens state control over voting systems. The ads signal rare intra-party friction on the issue. Meanwhile, Fox News reports that Kamala Harris's recent travel schedule and public statements point clearly toward a 2028 presidential run, though Harris has made no formal announcement.

sources.title: In fight against ICE facemasks, Black Democrats point to history
New ad campaign speaks out against Trump’s threat to ‘federalize’ elections
Kamala Harris' travels and comments clearly point to 2028

au-politics.headline: Australia's Fuel Crisis: Assurances Flow as Prices Mislead
au-politics.body:
Japan and other Asian nations have assured Australia that fuel supplies will continue normally, as Prime Minister Albanese works to contain a shortage that has rattled consumers and businesses. Farmers are among the hardest hit, with some reporting they cannot cover basic costs as fuel prices bite into already thin margins.

In New South Wales, regulators have fined nearly 100 service stations $1,100 each for displaying misleading prices during the crunch. Western Australia is moving to expand its FuelWatch scheme, requiring all regional stations to report prices from May - a transparency push that mirrors the pressure building on retailers nationally.

sources.title: 'Struggling massively': Farmer finds it hard to put food on the table
Nearly 100 NSW service stations fined $1,100 each over misleading petrol prices amid fuel shortage crackdown
Asian countries assure Australia ‘normal supply’ of fuel will continue as Albanese focuses on averting shortages
Japan assures Australia will get 'normal supply' of fuel
All WA regional stations must report fuel prices from May as FuelWatch scheme expands

au-politics.headline: Victoria Police Sued Over Officer Neal Thompson's Death
au-politics.body:
The sisters of Senior Constable Neal Thompson, killed by Dezi Freeman, plan to sue Victoria Police for negligence. Their lawyer says the family feels abandoned by the force, arguing police failed in their duty of care before the fatal incident.

The case puts institutional accountability at the centre of a public debate about how police manage known risks to their own officers. Victoria Police has not yet responded publicly to the legal action. The lawsuit is separate from any criminal proceedings against Freeman and will test what obligations the force owes to its members.

sources.title: Sisters of officer killed by Dezi Freeman plan to sue police for negligence, lawyer says
'Abandoned': Sisters of officer slain by Dezi Freeman to sue Victoria Police

au-politics.headline: Murder Arrest at Melbourne Airport; Tasmania Police Shooting
au-politics.body:
A man was arrested at Melbourne Airport while attempting to board an international flight, and has since been charged with murdering a woman. The arrest prevented what police believe was an attempt to flee the country.

Separately in Tasmania, a man is in serious condition after being shot by police in Goodwood, southern Tasmania. Details of the circumstances remain limited, with an investigation underway. Both incidents add to a weekend of serious law enforcement events across the country.

sources.title: Man arrested attempting to board international flight at Melbourne airport charged with murdering woman
Man in serious condition after being shot by Tasmania Police

au-politics.headline: Easter Weekend: Sport, Tragedy and Celebrations Across Australia
au-politics.body:
Tim Tszyu claimed a boxing title in Wollongong with a dominant performance over Denis Nurja, delivering a rare piece of good news for Australian sport on a weekend that also saw the Western Bulldogs defeat Essendon while losing two players to injury, and the Sydney Kings win a dramatic NBL overtime game - then break the trophy.

The weekend carried darker moments. A seven-year-old girl died after an ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east. A magnitude-5.5 earthquake shook a remote APY Lands community early in the morning. Australians otherwise marked Easter with celebrations, and travellers welcomed the reopening of a major highway closed by Cyclone Narelle.

sources.title: 7yo girl dies after ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east
Tim Tszyu dominates Denis Nurja to claim title in Wollongong
Australians find joy amid global uncertainty in Easter celebrations
Bulldogs down plucky Bombers but lose two key players to injury
Raiders' season lurches towards disaster as Knights run riot
Kings break NBL trophy after dramatic overtime win as 36ers blow late lead
Travellers celebrate as major highway reopens after Cyclone Narelle
Magnitude-5.5 earthquake 'morning wake-up call' to remote community

world.headline: US-Iran War Enters Day 37 as Conflict Spreads Regionally
world.body:
American and Israeli strikes on Iran entered their 37th day, with Iran retaliating by hitting power and water infrastructure in Kuwait. The conflict is widening fast. Thousands rallied in Baghdad against what protesters called senseless aggression, while Yemen's Houthis claimed a cluster missile strike on Ben Gurion airport.

Trump confirmed the rescue of a second downed US pilot inside Iranian territory, calling it miraculous. The F-15E crew recoveries offer a rare piece of good news for Washington as domestic opposition to the war grows - polls show the conflict is deeply unpopular with American voters. Trump is now said to be seeking an exit that avoids the appearance of outright failure.

sources.title: US pilot from downed F-15E plane rescued in Iran: What we know
What we know so far about rescue of US airman in Iran
Thousands rally in Iraq against ‘senseless’ US-Israel war on Iran
Trump seeks to end the war without it becoming ‘an abject failure’
Iran war: What is happening on day 37 of US-Israeli attacks?
Yemen’s Houthis claim cluster missile attack on Israel’s Ben Gurion airport
‘Miraculous’: US rescues second pilot downed in Iran, Trump confirms
Trump’s unpopular war on Iran

world.headline: Iran Offers Peace Roadmap While Squeezing the Strait of Hormuz
world.body:
Former Iranian Foreign Minister Zarif floated a peace roadmap even as Iran's military actions continued to rattle the Gulf. Gulf states responded coolly, citing deep erosion of trust. Iran separately signaled it would allow Iraqi and some other vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, while Oman held talks with Tehran aimed at keeping transit lanes open.

The diplomatic maneuvering reflects Iran's dual strategy - keeping economic pressure on Gulf neighbors through selective access to Hormuz while dangling the prospect of negotiations. Italy's prime minister, visiting the region, called the Gulf fundamental to European security, underlining how far the conflict's economic consequences now reach.

sources.title: Iran’s ex-FM Zarif proposes peace roadmap; Gulf points at erosion of trust
Italian PM says the Gulf is ‘fundamental’ for Europe’s security
Oman, Iran discuss smooth transit in Strait of Hormuz, Muscat says
Kuwait’s power, water plants damaged as Iran keeps attacking Gulf states
Iran says Iraqi ships can pass Strait of Hormuz as transits tick up

world.headline: Ukraine Strikes Deep Into Russian Oil Infrastructure
world.body:
Ukrainian drones hit a port in Russia's Primorsk and an oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod, sparking fires and reported fuel leaks. The strikes mark some of Ukraine's deepest hits on Russian energy infrastructure in recent weeks.

The attacks are part of Kyiv's sustained campaign to degrade Russian fuel supply chains and export capacity. Nizhny Novgorod sits roughly 400 kilometers east of Moscow, signaling an extended operational reach for Ukrainian drone forces.

sources.title: Ukraine hits port in Russia’s Primorsk, oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod

world.headline: Canadian Astronaut Hansen Heads to the Moon
world.body:
Jeremy Hansen became the first Canadian to travel to the moon, joining NASA's Artemis program as it pushes forward with crewed lunar missions. Hansen's inclusion marks a milestone for Canada's space program and reflects the increasingly multinational character of deep-space exploration.

The mission adds a rare note of international cooperation to a global news cycle otherwise dominated by conflict and economic strain.

sources.title: Jeremy Hansen is the first Canadian to head to the moon

ai.headline: AI Tools Blur Authorship as Fakes and Confusion Multiply
ai.body:
Grammarly's AI writing tools are producing what The Verge calls a 'sloppelganger' problem - text that sounds like you but isn't, eroding trust in your own voice over time. Separately, a folk musician found herself targeted by AI-generated fakes of her work alongside a copyright troll exploiting the chaos, illustrating how the same technology that blurs authorship also creates new legal vulnerabilities for small creators.

Andrej Karpathy published an example 'idea file' using an LLM wiki format, prompting discussion on Hacker News about how AI tools are quietly reshaping how people think and record knowledge. One essayist put it bluntly: the real threat is not AI taking over, but users drifting into not understanding what they are doing.

sources.title: Grammarly’s sloppelganger saga
A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
LLM Wiki – example of an "idea file"
The threat is comfortable drift toward not understanding what you're doing

ai.headline: Anthropic Adds Fees as Microsoft Drowns in Copilot Branding
ai.body:
Anthropic confirmed that Claude Code subscribers will pay extra to use OpenClaw, tightening the cost structure around its developer-focused AI tools just as competition in the coding-assistant market intensifies. The move signals that the era of bundled AI access is ending fast.

Meanwhile, a new analysis counted the number of distinct Microsoft products carrying the Copilot name and found the branding has sprawled into near-incoherence. The confusion is not just cosmetic - it reflects genuine fragmentation in Microsoft's AI strategy, where different Copilot products run on different models, have different capabilities, and target different users with little to distinguish them publicly.

sources.title: Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage
How many products does Microsoft have named 'Copilot'?

ai.headline: Security Stumbles: CBP Codes Leak, Google Locks Out Users
ai.body:
Sensitive U.S. Customs and Border Protection facility codes appear to have leaked through online flashcard sets, likely created by employees studying for internal certifications. Ars Technica found the codes sitting in publicly accessible study decks. It is a reminder that human habits routinely outpace institutional security controls.

On the consumer side, a detailed account of a Google Workspace account suspension went viral on Hacker News, describing how a paying user lost access to years of data with little recourse and opaque appeals. Germany's eIDAS digital identity rollout adds another wrinkle: its implementation will require users to have an Apple or Google account to function, handing two American platforms a mandatory role in European digital identity infrastructure.

sources.title: CBP facility codes sure seem to have leaked via online flashcards
Sad Story of My Google Workspace Account Suspension
German implementation of eIDAS will require an Apple/Google account to function

ai.headline: Linux 7.0 Halves PostgreSQL Speed, Ubuntu Bloat Grows
ai.body:
An AWS engineer reported that Linux 7.0 cuts PostgreSQL performance roughly in half under certain workloads, and a fix is not straightforward. The regression is significant for cloud infrastructure teams running high-throughput databases, and it arrives at a moment when kernel upgrades are often treated as routine.

Separately, Ubuntu's latest release now requires more RAM than Windows 11 to run, a threshold that would have seemed absurd a few years ago. The two stories together point to a broader trend: open-source software, long celebrated for running lean, is accumulating weight at both the OS and kernel level in ways that carry real operational costs.

sources.title: AWS engineer reports PostgreSQL perf halved by Linux 7.0, fix may not be easy
Ubuntu now requires more RAM than Windows 11

btc.headline: North Korea Spent Six Months Infiltrating Drift Before $280M Heist
btc.body:
Drift Protocol has confirmed that the $270-280 million exploit targeting its platform was a deliberate North Korean intelligence operation spanning roughly six months. Attackers embedded themselves inside the project before executing the theft, suggesting a level of patience and sophistication far beyond typical crypto hacks.

The findings point to state-sponsored actors treating DeFi protocols as strategic targets rather than opportunistic ones. For the broader industry, the implication is stark: perimeter security alone is insufficient when adversaries are willing to play a long game from the inside.

sources.title: Drift says $270 million exploit was a six-month North Korean intelligence operation
Drift Protocol $280M exploit took 'months of deliberate preparation'

btc.headline: Bitcoin Stuck at $67K as Bearish Sentiment Hits Multi-Week High
btc.body:
Bitcoin is grinding sideways around $67,000 while social media bearishness hits a five-week peak, according to Santiment data. Sentiment has deteriorated to its worst levels since the Iran war began, yet price has held relatively firm - a dynamic that contrarian traders sometimes read as a floor signal.

Not everyone is buying that logic. At least one prominent trader argues new lows are a matter of time if Bitcoin cannot break out of its current range. The tension between stubborn price support and collapsing crowd confidence is defining the market's mood heading deeper into April.

sources.title: Bitcoin holds steady as sentiment hits worst levels since Iran war began
New Bitcoin price lows 'matter of time' says trader with BTC stuck at $67K
Bitcoin bearish social chatter reaches 5-week high: Santiment

btc.headline: Crypto's Token Glut Is an Existential Problem, Analysts Warn
btc.body:
Token supply is growing faster than the value those tokens represent, and analysts are calling it an existential threat to the asset class. New issuance has become trivially cheap, flooding markets with projects that dilute attention and capital without delivering real utility.

The problem compounds during flat markets. When price appreciation masks weak fundamentals, the glut is tolerable. When it doesn't, investors are left holding tokens with no clear reason to exist. The industry has long promised to separate signal from noise - so far, supply keeps winning.

sources.title: Crypto faces ‘existential’ token problem as supply outpaces value creation

btc.headline: Ant Group and Quantum Threats Push Crypto's Infrastructure Frontier
btc.body:
Ant Group's blockchain arm has launched a platform letting AI agents transact autonomously over crypto rails, positioning programmable money as the settlement layer for machine-to-machine commerce. The move reflects growing conviction that AI and blockchain infrastructure will converge sooner than most expected.

Meanwhile, Bitcoin faces a longer-horizon threat. Researchers and developers are racing to quantum-proof the $1.3 trillion network before advances in quantum computing can crack its cryptographic foundations. Several initiatives are now underway, though no consensus has formed around a single solution. Both stories point to the same underlying pressure: the infrastructure built in crypto's early years was not designed for what the technology is becoming.

sources.title: Ant Group’s blockchain arm unveils platform for AI agents to transact on crypto rails
Bitcoin's $1.3 trillion security race: Key initiatives aimed at quantum-proofing the world's largest blockchain

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leeks with Soft-Boiled Eggs
food.description: Charred leeks brushed with a sweet-savory miso butter, served over rice with jammy eggs and toasted sesame
food.tip: Halve the leeks lengthwise and press them cut-side down in a dry pan until deeply caramelized before adding the glaze — the char is the whole point

exercise.name: The Sunday Carry
exercise.equipment: Two heavy bags, backpack, or loaded shopping bags
exercise.description: A farmer's carry walk around your neighborhood or park, alternating grip styles and distances to build functional grip strength, posture, and quiet cardio without a gym

learn.description: Stochastic resonance is the counterintuitive phenomenon where adding a precise amount of random noise to a weak signal actually makes that signal easier to detect — used by the nervous system, crayfish, and now medical devices
learn.why: It quietly dismantles the assumption that noise is always the enemy of clarity, and has surprising implications for how your own brain processes faint sensory information
